title: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency
preview:
From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Date: 2026-08-20 09:12 -0700
Subject: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Team —

Monitoring alerted at 03:08 UTC to a sustained p95 tail latency increase for model alpha-9 serving on our hosted fleet. Summary: p95 has drifted from ~420ms baseline to 1.05s over the last 4 hours (traffic-weighted), with increased variance on longer prompts. The spike correlates with the alpha-9 rollout 02:45 UTC and a kernel change in the optimized attention path.

Quick findings (attachments included):
- 03:00–06:00 UTC: gradual ramp, 06:00–09:00 UTC: step increase coincident with a subset of requests hitting the new kv-cache path.
- Affected tenants: 12 customers in US region; Acme Health shows elevated client-side timeouts on 0.7% of requests (attached sample traces).
- Reproduction locally on a sample workload shows p95 ~1.1s vs alpha-8 p95 ~430ms.

Mitigation options (tradeoffs):
1) Immediate: \"pin\" affected tenants to alpha-8 variant (no model code change) and apply a temporary traffic cap for alpha-9 for those tenants (recommended cap: 60% routing to alpha-9). Low-risk for correctness, moderate customer-visible behavior change.
2) Rollback alpha-9 globally to alpha-8 (broad rollback). Fast but disruptive to rollout metrics and to other tenants already benefiting from alpha-9 improvements.
3) Kernel patch hotfix (engineering) — likely takes 6+ hours to validate and deploy.

Request: I need authorization from sales/ops leadership to enact option #1 for Acme Health and the most impacted tenants as an emergency mitigation while we investigate. This will temporarily alter routing (affects latency SLOs coverage and may trigger an SLA exception for impacted time window).

Attachments: alpha9-tail-traces.tar.gz, alpha9-profiles.csv

If approved, I will: (a) pin Acme Health to alpha-8, (b) set traffic cap to 60% for alpha-9 for the next 12 hours, (c) continue trace collection and produce a short hotfix plan.

Rishi
Rishi Malhotra, Senior SRE
Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 09:34 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Thanks Rishi — this looks urgent. A couple quick Qs before I loop in legal/finance if needed:
- Can you confirm whether pinning to alpha-8 will change model outputs for any of Acme Health's anchored prompts (we need to avoid regressions for their clinical scoring flows)?
- Do we have a precise list of the 12 tenants (I only want to authorize for the small set that are showing client-side timeouts) — please paste IDs in a follow-up.
- I prefer option #1 if delta in outputs is minimal. Ben — can you pre-approve an emergency routing policy for Acme Health for a 2-hour mitigation window while we triage? We can extend if necessary but want an initial guardrail.

Ava Chen
Account Executive — Enterprise
Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 10:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Approve option #1 for Acme Health and the other tenants with confirmed client-side timeouts. Conditions:
- Keep the initial window to 2 hours and notify the customer immediately (Lauren, please send a short note).
- If the mitigation is still required after 2 hours, escalate back to Ben/Finance for a formal SLA exception.

Ava — you have my sign-off for the emergency routing policy for ACME-2345 for the 2-hour mitigation window. Rishi — proceed with pin + 60% cap for those tenant IDs once you paste them.

Ben Carter
Sales — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 10:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Timothy —

We're aware of increased tail latency impacting a small percentage of inference calls made by Acme Health this morning. Our engineering team has identified the likely cause and proposed a short mitigation that reduces exposure while they finish a fix. With your permission we'll:
- Temporarily route Acme Health traffic to the previous model variant for a short window, and
- Apply a conservative traffic cap to the new variant to reduce the chance of timeouts on your production calls.

We expect this to be a brief measure (initially 2 hours). Will this window work for your on-call/team? If you'd prefer we can schedule a similar maintenance window instead.

Apologies for the disruption — we'll follow up with a post-mortem and traces.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com

Thanks,
Rishi Malhotra

rishi_malhotra@redwood.com

title: Polaris POC: scheduling hands-on throughput dry run and prompt/routing review
SLO measurement & remedies — BlueBridge follow-up
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@bluebridge.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20T15:10:00-07:00
Subject: SLO measurement & remedies — BlueBridge follow-up

Hi Elena,

Thanks for the productive call earlier today. Quick recap and a few follow-ups so we can finalize the SLO wording for the technical and legal teams:

- Desired targets we discussed:
  • Streaming: time-to-first-byte (TTFB) p95 <= 150ms; p99 <= 400ms
  • Non-streaming (complete response): p95 <= 350ms; p99 <= 900ms
  • Throughput: sustain 50 req/s steady-state with bursts to 120 req/s for short windows
  • Concurrency: support 100 concurrent open WebSocket streams without queueing

- Clarifications we need from your side:
  1) Do you want streaming latency measured to first token only, or to first stable chunk (i.e., excluding initial protocol handshake)?
  2) For throughput, should we report requests/sec, tokens/sec, or both? Any preferred aggregation windows (1m, 5m)?
  3) Expected tokens per request distribution (median/95th) — we have a few sample payloads but would like to validate.

Attached is a first-pass SLO draft (bluebridge_slo_draft_20260820.pdf) that encodes the measurements we discussed and proposes exclusion language for scheduled maintenance and force-maj events.

Next steps I propose:
- Ben: share the draft with BlueBridge legal + point of contact for procurement (today)
- Marissa: prepare a short technical appendix on measurement method + synthetic canaries (by EOD tomorrow)
- Schedule: 30–45 minute technical deep-dive next week to validate tokenization/streaming behavior

If that all looks right, I’ll route the draft to your legal team and set up the deep-dive.

Best,
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
(415) 555-0142


From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@bluebridge.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Michael Chen <mchen@bluebridge.ai>
Date: 2026-08-20T18:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: SLO measurement & remedies — BlueBridge follow-up

On Thu, Aug 20, 2026 at 3:10 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the productive call earlier today. Quick recap and a few follow-ups so we can finalize the SLO wording for the technical and legal teams:
>
> - Desired targets we discussed:
>   • Streaming: time-to-first-byte (TTFB) p95 <= 150ms; p99 <= 400ms
>   • ...

Thanks, Ben — appreciate the crisp recap.

Answers inline and a few notes from our side:

1) Streaming measurement: we care primarily about time-to-first-token (TTFT). Exclude the initial WebSocket negotiation and TLS handshake, but include application-level server processing time. If you prefer, define an instrumentation marker (server emits first-token timestamp) — that will be the canonical measurement.

2) Throughput reporting: please report both requests/sec (completed reqs) and tokens/sec aggregated over 1-minute windows. We will expect monthly averages plus 95th percentile of the 1-minute windows.

3) Tokens/request: median ~ 220 tokens, p95 ~ 850 tokens. Most calls are short prompts plus contextual history; occasionally we send longer batch inference for analytics.

A few contractual items we want reflected in the SLA language:
- Exclusions: planned maintenance windows (we’ll agree on a 72-hour rolling schedule), and emergency upgrades with 24-48 hour notice where possible.
- Cold-start/capacity ramp: we understand warm pools matter — we want a clear ramp policy so spikes during initial rollout aren’t counted against SLOs.
- Remedies: service credits are acceptable. Our procurement team prefers a credit formula tied to monthly invoice with a cap (we can discuss cap number), and a 30-day cure period for operational fixes before termination rights kick in.

We’re OK moving the draft to legal once the measurement definitions above are captured. Please CC Michael (our procurement lead) on the legal send.

— Elena

Elena Rossi
Head of Platform Engineering | BlueBridge Analytics
elena.rossi@bluebridge.ai
+1 617 555 0199


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-21T08:05:00-07:00
Subject: Draft SLA language and measurement appendix for BlueBridge

Ben/Evelyn — drafting notes based on Elena’s answers. This is wording we can feed to legal; I included a short technical appendix and remediation table.

Key definitions (proposed):
- Measurement windows: all percentiles and throughput numbers are computed over rolling 1-minute windows; monthly SLO achievement is measured over the calendar month.
- Streaming latency (TTFT): time from Redwood ingress to first application token emitted to the client; excludes TLS handshake and WebSocket upgrade; measured at server ingress timestamp vs first-token timestamp.
- Non-streaming latency: time from request ingress to final token emitted.
- Throughput: reported as completed requests/sec and tokens/sec, aggregated into 1-minute buckets; monthly report includes average and 95th percentile of those 1-minute buckets.
- Concurrency: number of simultaneously active requests/streams tracked at server ingress; spike handling and queuing behavior described in appendix.

Exclusions (proposed):
1) Scheduled maintenance windows agreed with 48–72 hours notice
2) Force majeure or third-party network outages (with reasonable evidence)
3) Customer-originated issues (invalid payloads, abusive requests)
4) Agreed capacity ramp events for initial rollout (documented ahead of time)

Reporting cadence and evidence:
- Weekly health snapshots emailed to BlueBridge point of contact
- Monthly SLA report (CSV + dashboard export) delivered within 5 business days of month end — includes percentile tables, sampled traces for tail events, and synthetic canary results
- On-demand access to raw aggregates via shared dashboard link (see related links)

Remedies (proposed):
- Service credit tiers: 1) <SLO threshold> = 5% credit; 2) material breach (two monthly misses in a quarter) = 15% credit; credits applied to next billing cycle; total credit cap at 25% of monthly invoice
- Cure period: Redwood to propose remediation plan within 30 days; if the same SLO is missed three months in a row, customer may elect contract termination

Attachments: bluebridge_slo_draft_20260820.pdf (has wording + appendix)
Drive draft link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2B-bluebridge-slo-drafts

I’ll turn this into a redline-ready doc for legal. Ben — I suggest you include Michael in the legal send and propose two times for a technical deep-dive next Tue/Wed.

Thanks,
Marissa

Marissa Cole
Director, Enterprise Sales Ops | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

Subject: RFI: Operator delegation & cloud operator safeguards

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking this RFI. As part of our security review for the upcoming contract we need a detailed mapping of any delegated operators and subprocessors that will handle customer data or manage infrastructure for Acme Financial. Specifically we need: 

- A current inventory of subprocessors (vendor name, role, region, data access scope).
- Any changes in the last 12 months and planned changes in the next 90 days.
- For cloud operator partners (regions/edge nodes), a description of safeguards in place (KMS key custody, access controls, breach notification SLA).
- Indication of which operators have access to production KVs or network admin privileges.

We would prefer a spreadsheet (vendor matrix) plus a short narrative explaining recent changes. Can you deliver the initial artifacts by EOD Wednesday (2026-08-20)? Our SOC2 auditors will be reviewing this, so redacted evidence pointers (SOC2 report location, attestation letters, shared drive links) are helpful.

Also note: we will share a follow-up with a preferred template if that helps.

Appreciate the quick turnaround—this RFI is gating our legal team's draft.

Best,
Olivia Park
Head of Security Operations
Acme Financial

Subject: Internal: Acme RFI — owner assignments and deliverables

Ben / team,

Folks — below is the breakdown I propose to keep the RFI tight and auditable. Please confirm on your lines: 

- Soojin: canonical subprocessor roster (CSV) — include vendor legal name, procurement POC if available, role (e.g., telemetry processing, cloud operator, monitoring), region, last changed date, and whether they have encryption key access.
- Karthik: cloud operator safeguards memo — include KMS custody model, any HSM/KMS integrations (customer-managed vs Redwood-managed), cross-region failover and formal notification windows for capacity changes.

Laura Bennett
Senior Manager, Trust & Safety Operations
Redwood Inference
attachments: subprocessor_roster.csv (working draft)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Park <olivia.park@acmefinancial.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@acmefinancial.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20T16:43:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Operator delegation & cloud operator safeguards — Redwood response

Hi Olivia / Acme team,

Attached is the package we discussed: vendor_matrix_v3.xlsx, subprocessor_roster.csv (final working copy), and a short operator delegation mapping PDF that summarizes who has which administrative privileges and the safeguards in place. High-level summary: 

- New third-party operator: CloudOps Partners LLC (role: GCP data-plane operator in eu-west1). Added on 2026-06-15; access is limited to compute orchestration and does not include KMS key access.
- KMS custody: Redwood uses a split custody model — customer-managed KMS is supported for Dedicated & Private deployments; for Hosted customers we use a Redwood-managed KMS with strict role-based access and HSM-backed key storage. KMS access is limited to our crypto ops team and requires multi-admin approval.
- Region fallbacks & routing: we maintain a documented fallback matrix (attached PDF) — requests are routed by policy and will failover to region-compatible operator pools with automatic notifications to affected customers.
- Access control: only a small set of named operator accounts have production network admin privileges; those accounts are audited daily and require ticketing justification for SSH/RDP access.

Evidence pointers included in the package: link to SOC 2 attestation (summary), operator mapping PDF with timestamps, and the CSV vendor roster. If your auditors need the full SOC 2 report, reply and I will send a secure link.

Next steps: 1) Please confirm receipt and that format meets your auditors' needs. 2) If you want a short 30-minute walkthrough of the package with Karthik and Soojin, we can book tomorrow morning.

Best regards,
Ben Carter
Director, Customer Solutions
Redwood Inference

title: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@nexview.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 14:12:00 -0400
Subject: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Hi Daniel / Elena —

Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed owners so we have a single source of truth for the POC plan. High level we heard:

- Goal: Validate real-time summarization + ranked insights for clinical notes (latency target < 200ms for tail, throughput ~120 req/s during peak).
- Primary constraints: PHI-handling requirements, VPC-only traffic, and a desire to run embeddings & reranker as part of the pipeline.

Proposed technical checklist and owners (please confirm):

1) SDK & auth setup (owner: Jordan)
   - Setup service account with scoped API keys and VPC egress rules. Evaluate short-lived tokens vs. static key + KMS rotation.
2) Routing & model variant policy (owner: Rishi)
   - Route high-priority traffic to a low-latency variant; failover to cost-optimized model on saturation.
3) Embeddings + reranker (owner: NexView + Redwood eng)
   - Store embeddings in your existing vector DB (we’ll provide embedding profiles).
4) Structured output / tool-calling (owner: Ava)
   - Define tool schema for lookup calls (patient lookup, medication mapping). We recommend explicit schema + strict type checking to reduce hallucination.
5) Prompt governance & safety (owner: Marissa + NexView Sec)
   - Versioned system prompts, injection mitigations (context window filters, whitelist/blacklist of tools), and a red-team plan.

Attachments: I’ve attached a one-page SOW and a first-pass architecture surface for discussion. Also included a small set of starter prompt templates (prompt-templates-v0.2.md).

Next steps / proposed timeline:
- Jordan: send SDK onboarding steps and credential template by EOD Thu (20 Aug).
- Rishi: share model routing policy examples and perf targets by Fri (21 Aug).
- NexView: provide sample anonymized notes + expected QPS by Mon (24 Aug).
- Marissa & Ava: organize a red-team kickoff for prompt injection tests (week of 8/31).

If anything above is off, flag it here. Otherwise I’ll update the HubSpot record and drive folder to reflect these owners.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachment: nexview-integration-sow-v1.pdf (PDF, 1 page); arch-surface-plan.drawio.png (PNG); prompt-templates-v0.2.md (MD)

From: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@nexview.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Thanks Ava — this is excellent and aligns with what we discussed. A few clarifications from our side: 

- For PHI, our security team requires token rotation and HSM-backed keys for any persistent secrets. Short-lived tokens with a refresh flow are preferred.
- Vector DB: we plan to use our existing FAISS cluster behind the VPN. Can Redwood’s embeddings profile be normalized to match our F32 layout (we currently store 1536-d floats)?
- Tooling: we like the explicit schema approach. For the POC we only need patient lookup and a simple ICD mapping tool.

We’ll upload a small anonymized dataset by Monday (24 Aug). I attached a quick doc that lists the PHI handling checklist from our security review.

Two quick asks:
1) Can Jordan provide the SDK quickstart that shows service-account creation and least-privilege roles?
2) Can Rishi share any example routing JSON for low-latency vs. cost-fallback so our infra team can pre-approve network policies?

Appreciate it —
Daniel Park
Head of Data Platform, NexView Analytics
Daniel.Park@nexview.ai | +1 (646) 212-3300

Attachment: nexview-security-checklist.pdf (PDF, 2 pages)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 11:28:00 -0400
Subject: SDK onboarding & credential patterns

Hi Daniel —

I put together a short SDK quickstart and credential template and dropped it into the shared Drive (link below). Highlights:

- We support two onboarding flows: service account (recommended for VPC/private deployments) and short-lived OAuth tokens (recommended for hosted, low-friction demos).
- For your PHI requirement I recommend service account + KMS-wrapped API key with a 24-hour rotation window. We can integrate with your HSM for signing if needed.
- Example Terraform snippets included for binding least-privilege roles and creating VPC egress rules.

Quickstart link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bNexViewPOC (README: SDK-quickstart.md)

I’ll also attach a JSON example of a routing policy; Rishi — tagging you for the model variant perf numbers.

—Jordan
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Jordan.Blake@redwood.com

Attachment: SDK-quickstart.md (MD); credential-template.json (JSON)

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:15:00 -0400
Subject: Re: SDK onboarding & credential patterns / routing examples

Folks —

Posting an example routing policy here (abbreviated) and the perf budget we used in other clinical POCs. Full JSON is in the Drive.

- Low-latency route: model_variant: redwood-llama-13b-rt
  - Target p99: 180ms (tokenization included)
  - Batching: continuous batch window 5ms, max batch 16
- Cost-fallback route: model_variant: redwood-13b-opt
  - Switch when queue length > 60 or median latency > 250ms

Embedding profile: we export 1536-d float32 vectors by default. We can add a normalization flag to match your storage layout.

Prompt safety notes (quick):
- System prompts: store in versioned namespace (prompt:v0.1, prompt:v0.2). Each change must reference a changelog entry and approval from security.
- Injection mitigations: context sanitizers (strip user-injected tool calls), tool whitelist enforcement in the runtime, and per-request policy tags to disable unknown tools.
- Red-team plan: two-week focused attack window post-POC where we will run adversarial prompts, then produce a remediation backlog.

Next asks:
- Daniel / Elena: confirm vector format (1536 float32 ok?) and provide sample throughput profile.
- Marissa: I’ll sync with you on the prompt versioning workflow — do you prefer Git-based prompts or a UI in Console?

Thanks,
Rishi
Staff Engineer, Inference Runtime, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 22:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Thanks everyone — solid progress. A couple of governance items before we lock the POC scope: 

1) Prompt versioning: we prefer a Git-backed flow with signed commits for production system prompts. For the POC, we can accept a staged Console UI (with audit logs) but production must be Git-first.
2) Injection testing: Redwood will run an initial red-team pass, but NexView security must approve the test plan. We’ll share the attack vectors doc and proposed cadence by Friday.
3) Acceptance criteria:
   - No high-severity hallucinations on the sample dataset (as defined in our eval rubric).
   - Embedding cosine recall > 0.78 for top-5 retrievals on anonymized test set.

Action items recap (owners and due dates):
- Jordan: SDK quickstart + credential template — Thu 20 Aug (done).
- Rishi: routing JSON + perf targets — Fri 21 Aug (drive link).
- NexView (Daniel): sample anonymized notes + throughput profile — Mon 24 Aug.
- Marissa + NexView Sec: review red-team test plan — Fri 21 Aug.

I’ll update the HubSpot deal and schedule the red-team kickoff once the sample dataset lands.

—Marissa Cole
Enterprise Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

Daniel Park
Head of Data Platform, NexView Analytics
Daniel.Park@nexview.ai | +1 (646) 212-3300

Attachment: nexview-security-checklist.pdf (PDF, 2 pages)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 11:28:00 -0400
Subject: SDK onboarding & credential patterns

Hi Daniel —

I put together a short SDK quickstart and credential template and dropped it into the shared Drive (link below). Highlights:

- We support two onboarding flows: service account (recommended for VPC/private deployments) and short-lived OAuth tokens (recommended for hosted, low-friction demos).
- For your PHI requirement I recommend service account + KMS-wrapped API key with a 24-hour rotation window. We can integrate with your HSM for signing if needed.
- Example Terraform snippets included for binding least-privilege roles and creating VPC egress rules.

Quickstart link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bNexViewPOC (README: SDK-quickstart.md)

I’ll also attach a JSON example of a routing policy; Rishi — tagging you for the model variant perf numbers.

—Jordan
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Jordan.Blake@redwood.com

Attachment: SDK-quickstart.md (MD); credential-template.json (JSON)

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:15:00 -0400
Subject: Re: SDK onboarding & credential patterns / routing examples

Folks —

Posting an example routing policy here (abbreviated) and the perf budget we used in other clinical POCs. Full JSON is in the Drive.

- Low-latency route: model_variant: redwood-llama-13b-rt
  - Target p99: 180ms (tokenization included)
  - Batching: continuous batch window 5ms, max batch 16
- Cost-fallback route: model_variant: redwood-13b-opt
  - Switch when queue length > 60 or median latency > 250ms

Embedding profile: we export 1536-d float32 vectors by default. We can add a normalization flag to match your storage layout.

Prompt safety notes (quick):
- System prompts: store in versioned namespace (prompt:v0.1, prompt:v0.2). Each change must reference a changelog entry and approval from security.
- Injection mitigations: context sanitizers (strip user-injected tool calls), tool whitelist enforcement in the runtime, and per-request policy tags to disable unknown tools.
- Red-team plan: two-week focused attack window post-POC where we will run adversarial prompts, then produce a remediation backlog.

Next asks:
- Daniel / Elena: confirm vector format (1536 float32 ok?) and provide sample throughput profile.
- Marissa: I’ll sync with you on the prompt versioning workflow — do you prefer Git-based prompts or a UI in Console?

Thanks,
Rishi
Staff Engineer, Inference Runtime, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 22:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Thanks everyone — solid progress. A couple of governance items before we lock the POC scope: 

1) Prompt versioning: we prefer a Git-backed flow with signed commits for production system prompts. For the POC, we can accept a staged Console UI (with audit logs) but production must be Git-first.
2) Injection testing: Redwood will run an initial red-team pass, but NexView security must approve the test plan. We’ll share the attack vectors doc and proposed cadence by Friday.
3) Acceptance criteria:
   - No high-severity hallucinations on the sample dataset (as defined in our eval rubric).
   - Embedding cosine recall > 0.78 for top-5 retrievals on anonymized test set.

Action items recap (owners and due dates):
- Jordan: SDK quickstart + credential template — Thu 20 Aug (done).
- Rishi: routing JSON + perf targets — Fri 21 Aug (drive link).
- NexView (Daniel): sample anonymized notes + throughput profile — Mon 24 Aug.
- Marissa + NexView Sec: review red-team test plan — Fri 21 Aug.

I’ll update the HubSpot deal and schedule the red-team kickoff once the sample dataset lands.

—Marissa Cole
Enterprise Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

title: Preferred invoice intake & PO stamping for North America vendor onboarding
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Peters <jordan@acme-supplies.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Preferred invoice intake & PO stamping for North America vendor onboarding

Hi Jordan —

Congrats on getting through vendor setup — happy to have Acme Supplies live. A couple quick items Finance wants clarified before the first invoice:

1) Preferred intake: we operate a vendor portal (link below) and prefer invoices uploaded there. That gives faster BO processing and automated PO matching.
2) Email fallback: we can accept invoices by email only when the attachment includes the exact PO number in the filename and the PDF is searchable (not a scanned image). If you must email, please use ap@redwood.com and include the PO in both subject and filename.
3) PO stamping / matching: Finance needs invoices to contain the Redwood PO number in a prominent field (Invoice header or line-item comments). If your system can add a \"PO: Redwood-12345\" stamp on PDF export, that is ideal.

I'm attaching our preferred invoice template and an example PO PDF. If anything in the template causes friction for your ERP, let me know and we can route it to our onboarding ops to make exceptions.

Next step: can you confirm if Acme will upload to the portal or prefer the email fallback? If email, confirm whether PDFs are born-digital.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 415-555-0182

Attachments: redwood_invoice_template_v1.xlsx (xlsx), PO-example-ACME-12345.pdf (pdf)

Links: Vendor portal: https://portal.redwood.com/vendor-onboard


From: Jordan Peters <jordan@acme-supplies.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: AP Team <ap@acme-supplies.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 13:33:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Preferred invoice intake & PO stamping for North America vendor onboarding

Hi Ben —

Thanks — we can do portal uploads starting next week. A couple of follow-ups from our side:

- Our ERP can add PO numbers to the filename and include them on the invoice header, yes.
- The PDF output is generated from our billing system (born-digital). No scanned PDFs.
- One ask: can Redwood accept a 6-digit internal PO suffix? Our system appends a 6-digit batch ID to your PO (e.g., Redwood-12345-000321). Will that still match on your end?

If not, we can toggle to a clean PO-only filename, but that requires coordination with our billing schedule.

Jordan Peters
AP Manager, Acme Supplies
jordan@acme-supplies.com

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Peters <jordan@acme-supplies.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 08:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Preferred invoice intake & PO stamping for North America vendor onboarding

Jordan —

Quick Finance note: we can support the 6-digit suffix but only if the root PO (Redwood-12345) appears verbatim somewhere in the filename or the invoice header metadata. If your filenames will be Redwood-12345-000321.pdf, Finance's ingest will match. If the PO is obfuscated (e.g., ACME12345), it will fail automated matching and require manual routing, which delays payment.

Second, please ensure the invoice line-items include unit price and total (some of your previous invoices we received elsewhere were summary-only and required follow-up).

If you want, Laura (cc'd) can schedule a 15-min call with your billing team to verify the filename pattern and a sample PDF.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Finance Ops Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Peters <jordan@acme-supplies.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Preferred invoice intake & PO stamping for North America vendor onboarding

Perfect — thanks for the quick answers, Jordan. Portal upload next week works.

Summary and action items:
- Acme will upload via portal starting 2026-08-24.
- Email fallback allowed only if filename contains the root PO exactly (e.g., Redwood-12345 or Redwood-12345-000321). Use ap@redwood.com if needed.
- PDFs must be born-digital (not scanned) and include line-item detail + total.
- Attached: our template again for your billing team.

Laura will reach out to set a 15-min verification call (she's copied). If anything needs a formal exception (e.g., different filename policy), we'll capture it in the onboarding checklist and record the exception in the vendor profile.

Thanks all — looking forward to smooth first invoice processing.

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

title: Kite-XS variant: integration gap review & temporary version lock request
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T09:14:00Z
Subject: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Team —

We just got the vendor compatibility report from Nimbus for the Kite-XS 4B family (see attached). High level: the model appears to run fine in our runtime, but there are two areas where the variant diverges from our standard tokenizer and KV layout which could cause downstream hallucination/regression for a subset of prompt shapes.

Quick summary: 
- Tokenization differences: subtle BOS/EOS treatment on edge-case punctuation sequences.
- KV cache ordering: variant uses packed KV dims which changes memory layout assumptions in a few lower-level kernels.

Ask: before we let this variant into the public catalog, I want to request a temporary version lock (pin) for Kite-XS on the current vetted variant (v2.3.1) while Eng runs a short integration sweep and we stage a canary. Proposed window: 2026-08-20 -> 2026-09-15.

Please confirm feasibility and list the minimal telemetry/QA checks you need from Product to unblock. I attached the compat report and a draft canary plan.

Thanks —
Ava

Ava Chen
Product Manager, Model Infra
Redwood Inference


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-13T11:04:00Z
Subject: Re: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Ava —

Thanks for the write-up and the report. Read through the PDF and ran a short local pass on our staging infra. Concise notes:

- Tokenizer: we can normalize the BOS/EOS behavior on load with a tiny compatibility shim. Risk: ~0.2% tokenization divergence on long-chain punctuation tests.
- KV layout: this is the bigger one. Our current kernels expect a particular stride. We can add a fast-path that supports packed dims but it will need a micro-bench and a kernel bench job (2-3 dev days + a GPU bench slot).

Workaround proposal to enable the pin window quickly:
1) Temporary pin to v2.3.1 at routing layer so production requests use the vetted variant.
2) Deploy a staging canary (small % traffic) to the Kite-XS build behind a feature flag. Canary should collect: tokenization diffs, perplexity delta on our smoke evals, and tail-latency per-token.
3) Devs will deliver a kernel shim PR and a perf bench by 2026-08-26.

If Product signs off on the telemetry (we need the canonical prompt set + a short list of golden prompts from Nimbus), I can schedule the bench. Otherwise we risk re-introducing behavior changes into user-facing workloads.

I'll attach a short checklist in a follow-up.

— Dev

Dev Patel
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-15T15:29:00Z
Subject: Re: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Folks —

Agree with Dev's assessment. Operational ask from Reliability: if we do a temporary lock, we need a rollback runbook + a capacity plan for the pinned variant in the event Nimbus's build has different perf characteristics.

Canary suggestions:
- 0.5% production traffic for 48 hours, check: tokenization delta, 99th percentile latency, error rate (non-200).
- If any metric crosses the threshold (tokenization delta > 1.0% on canonical set OR tail latency +25%), auto-fallback to v2.3.1.

I created a short canary template and added it as kite-xs-canary-plan.md (attached). If legal/compliance needs to review retention for telemetry, loop them in.

Laura

Laura Bennett
Principal SRE
Redwood Inference


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Sarah Mitchell <sarah@nimbus.ai>
Date: 2026-08-18T09:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Quick product decision: we are comfortable with the proposed temporary routing lock to v2.3.1 for the public catalog while engineering runs the integration work, provided Nimbus supplies a short golden prompt set (10-15 prompts) and agrees to a 30-day post-entry telemetry review.

Sarah — can you confirm you can provide the golden set and a compact eval script by EOD 2026-08-20?

Action items I want tracked:
- Nimbus: provide golden prompts + eval script (Sarah)
- Eng (Dev): kernel shim PR + perf bench by 2026-08-26
- SRE (Laura): canary deployment + auto-fallback policy implemented before 2026-08-20
- Product (Ava): sign-off on telemetry thresholds and canary window (you own)

I will mark this as a provisional exception in the intake tracker pending the above.

— Lauren

Lauren Bishop
Director, Model Product
Redwood Inference


From: Sarah Mitchell <sarah@nimbus.ai>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Paul Torres <paul@cloudops.com>
Date: 2026-08-20T16:42:00Z
Subject: Re: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Thanks all — Nimbus will provide the 12 golden prompts and a lightweight eval harness by COB today (uploaded to the doc link Lauren referenced). We agree to the 30-day telemetry review and will stay on standby for any debugging.

Per your note on pinning, we accept the temporary routing lock to v2.3.1 for the public catalog through 2026-09-15. If your bench results indicate the shim is sufficient we can schedule a variant refresh; otherwise we'll coordinate a smaller patch release.

Attachments: kite-xs-compat-report.pdf (from Nimbus), kite-xs-canary-plan.md (SRE template)

Best,
Sarah Mitchell
Head of Partnerships
NimbusAI

title: Regional econ snapshot + next steps for FinChain residency decision
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
Cc: Andre Sousa <andre.sousa@finchain.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 17:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Emma / Andre —

Thanks again for the call today. Summarizing the key points and a very compact cost snapshot so we can align on a feasible pilot:

- Current target workload: 4–6M combined prompt+completion tokens / month (mostly short-form inference, 80% chat-style prompts).
- Desired target price band: ~$250–$350 per 1M tokens (goal: predictable $/1M for budgeting).
- Residency requirement: EU data residency for EU users (~15% traffic), with the remainder US. EU residency is a requirement for the upcoming Q4 launch.

Quick back-of-envelope (list prices, pre-discount):
- US hosted baseline: $250 / 1M tokens
- EU hosted premium: +20% => $300 / 1M tokens
- If effective cache/hit-rate optimizations apply (we typically see 15–30% token reduction on query-heavy apps), you can drop net spend by roughly the same percentage.

Example monthly scenarios (no discounts, no caching):
- 4M tokens: US = $1,000 / month; EU portion (600k tokens @ $300/1M) adds $180 -> blended ~$1,180
- 6M tokens: US = $1,500; EU portion adds $270 -> blended ~$1,770

Attachments: I added a short spreadsheet with scenario rows and a one-pager that outlines hosted vs dedicated economics and where reserved capacity makes sense.

Next steps I propose:
1) You confirm a firm pilot volume (e.g., 3mo at 5M/mo).
2) We run a quick technical checklist (latency SLOs, KV cache feasibility for your prompt patterns).
3) We provision a 30-day pilot quote that shows both US-only and US+EU pricing (with clear per-1M tokens line items).

Happy to jump on a quick 20-minute call to walk the sheet if that helps.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: regional-unit-econ-sheet.xlsx (spreadsheet)]
[Attachment: redwood-pricing-compact.pdf (one-page PDF)]

-----Original Message-----
From: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Andre Sousa <andre.sousa@finchain.com>
Date: Mon, 17 Aug 2026 09:30:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Ben —

Thanks for this — the calc format is helpful. Couple of clarifying items from our side:
1) Confirmed pilot target: leaning toward 5M tokens/month (we want a 90-day window to validate usage).
2) Budget: we have an initial target of $1,400–$2,200 / month for inference (this is a draft allocation from the platform budget).
3) Residency split: roughly 15% of requests must remain in EU jurisdiction; the rest can be US.

A few quick questions:
- What does the EU premium buy us operationally? Is it strictly region hosting, or are there different isolation/contractual terms?
- If we hit 20M tokens / month after Q1 next year, how do tiered unit prices typically change?

If you can drop a few scenarios at the 5M target with our budget in mind that would be great. Also copy our infra lead Andre — he'll want the latency and KV cache notes.

Emma Liu
Head of Platform | FinChain
[Signature]

-----Original Message-----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Emma — great, thanks for the clarity. Two quick answers and a scenario table inline: 

EU premium — what it covers:
- Physical region hosting + data residency (data written to disk and logs stay in the selected EU region).
- Contractual assurances for residency (we flag the EU tenancy on the invoice and on the contract appendix); available KMS/HSM integrations by region.
- Operational delta: slightly higher infra cost and inter-region network egress that drives the ~15–25% premium. No functional difference in the runtime.

Tiered unit price examples (list -> illustrative net):
- 1–5M / month: US list $250 / 1M; EU list $300 / 1M
- 5–20M / month: US list $215 / 1M; EU list $258 / 1M (we typically can push the per-1M lower with committed volume)
- 20M+ / month: US list $180 / 1M; EU list $216 / 1M

5M pilot scenarios (no discounts, then illustrative 20% commit discount):
- No discount, no caching: blended (85% US / 15% EU) = (4.25M*$250 + 0.75M*$300) / 5 = blended ~$262 / 1M => $1,310 / month
- With optimistic 20% reserved-commit discount: blended ~$210 / 1M => $1,050 / month
- With 20% caching reduction on tokens + 20% commit discount: net spend ~ $840 / month

So your stated budget band ($1,400–$2,200) maps comfortably to a 5M pilot under list pricing, and gives runway for both dedicated/reserved options if you want lower net unit cost.

Recommended next steps:
- Ben/Jordan: prepare a 30-day pilot quote with both hosted US-only and hosted US+EU (line items showing list and a 20% committed discount scenario).
- Technical: Marissa to run the quick KV cache feasibility checklist with Andre (I attached a short checklist file).

I can also draft a suggested commercial (12-mo commit with quarterly true-ups) if you want to see the committed math.

Best,
Ben

[Attachment: eu-residency-cost-notes.txt (plain text)]

-----Original Message: Internal note -----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: FinChain pilot options & proposed commercial approach

Quick internal summary — do not forward to customer: 
- Customer: FinChain (Emma / Andre). Pilot: 5M/mo for 90 days, 15% EU residency.
- Recommended commercial: offer two lanes -> (A) Hosted pilot (no commit) at list; (B) 3-month pilot with optional 12-mo commit that converts to a reserved capacity credit with a 20–30% blended discount at signing. Aim to get a 12-mo commit for lower CPI if they agree to projected growth.
- Pricing math to include: per-1M list (US/EU), reserve discount scenarios (15/20/30%), and an explicit row for expected caching uplift (conservative 15%, optimistic 30%).

Action items:
- Jordan: build the pilot quote (line-itemized by region, show both list and -20% commit).
- Marissa: start technical checklist with Andre (latency targets, KV cache feasibility, and any PII data flow notes for EU residency).
- I will propose the commercial term language and guardrails for stateful caching (no residency drift).

Thanks — Ben

title: Service-level economics: counterproposal and signature path
preview:
From: Adi Shah <adi.shah@solstice.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@solstice.ai>, Solstice Legal <legal@solstice.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SLA options and pricing — need a clearer final offer

Ben / Monica —

Thanks for the call this morning. We appreciated the walkthrough of the Dedicated plan and the explainers on routing/fallback behavior. Two asks from Procurement and Legal that came up after the internal review:

1) We need an explicit three-tier uptime schedule (99.5 / 99.9 / 99.95) with clear crediting math per month and a hard cap on total credits.
2) Response windows for Sev1 and Sev2 incidents to be contractually guaranteed for business hours and 24x7 respectively; procurement is pushing for a steeper credit if an incident is not acknowledged within 15 minutes.

Also, the discounts discussed on the call (15% at $100k ARR, 22% at $250k ARR) need to be documented in a counterproposal. Our CFO wants to see a final single-page economics summary we can take to the board by Thursday.

Can you share a clean counterproposal (pricing + SLA table) and the next steps for signature? If you can include a straightforward DocuSign path, that would help.

Thanks —
Adi
Head of Product, Solstice AI

—
Notice: This message may contain confidential information for the intended recipient only.

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Adi Shah <adi.shah@solstice.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@solstice.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 11:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Service-level economics: counterproposal and signature path

Adi —

Appreciate the clear asks. I routed these to finance and legal and put Monica and Laura on the thread to help scope the operational commitments. Attached is a compact counterproposal: SLA_Tiers_v2.pdf (summary table + crediting formula) and a redline-ready Master Agreement draft (Solstice_MA_for_Redwood.docx). High-level responses below:

- Uptime tiers: we can commit to 99.5 / 99.9 / 99.95 with credits as a fraction of monthly committed fee. The attached table includes a hard cap equal to 200% of one month’s committed fees.
- Acknowledgement & response times: our standard Sev1 acknowledgement is 15 minutes (24x7) when customer is on Premier support. For standard Dedicated customers, we do 30-minute acknowledgement for Sev1 business hours and 2 hours outside. Because you asked for a tighter 15-minute acknowledgement with specific financial remedies, we have a Premier Support add-on that includes 15-minute acknowledgement and an escalations engineer on-call for high-severity incidents. Pricing for Premier is in the attachment.
- Discount schedule: I modeled two options in the doc — a volume discount ladder (what we discussed) and a committed-spend tier that frontloads a portion of the discount with an annual commit. Both include a one-time onboarding credit to offset migration effort.

Next steps I propose:
1) You review the attached simplified SLA table — confirm which uptime tier and whether Premier Support is required.
2) If the board wants the most protection, we can produce a final MA with a Premier addendum for signature by Thursday; Laura can confirm the final invoice timing.
3) If approved, I’ll send a DocuSign envelope with a single-signature flow for Solstice (we can append Redwood signature after you sign).

I want to highlight one operational constraint so expectations are aligned: Premier Support requires a 12-month committed Reserved Capacity plan to guarantee latency corridors and the 15-minute acknowledgement SLAs stated. That constraint is reflected in the pricing table.

Attaching the files again for easy reference.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales Director | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: SLA_Tiers_v2.pdf (pdf), Solstice_MA_for_Redwood.docx (docx)

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Adi Shah <adi.shah@solstice.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@solstice.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 14:22:00 -07:00

Adi —

Quick technical note to help the board: Premier Support (detailed in the attachment) includes the following operational commitments beyond acknowledgement times:
- Dedicated GPU pool reservation (pinned nodes) to avoid noisy-neighbor variability
- Priority routing and dedicated failover lane (regional)
- Monthly health-check report and token-level latency percentiles

If you sign Premier, we’ll include a 60-day rollout window for capacity reservation and a joint runbook for incident handoffs. Happy to jump on a quick 20-min call to walk through the runbook with your ops team.

Monica Patel
Solutions Engineering Manager | Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwoodinference.com

On a separate note: Laura—can you confirm whether we can allow a 30-day invoice lag vs the standard 14-day for this customer to help with their board approval cadence?

Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:37:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Service-level economics: counterproposal and signature path

Hi all —

We can offer a one-time concession to 30-day invoice terms for the first 12 months if Solstice signs the 12-month Reserved Capacity commitment and accepts the Premier addendum. That will be included in the MA redline. The credit cap and the financial remedies as Ben outlined are acceptable to finance with those constraints.

I’ll prepare the final billing paragraph and confirm the invoice schedule in the DocuSign envelope.

Laura Bennett
VP, Finance | Redwood Inference

Two quick asks before we proceed to DocuSign: 1) Can you flag the exact numeric credit percentages in the SLA table (we want the board to see the $ impact, not just the formula), and 2) Can you confirm the timing of the Reserved Capacity start date relative to signature (we want to avoid a gap where we’re paying but capacity hasn’t been reserved).

title: SIG walkthrough and pre-call Qs for Cedarcrest
preview:
From: Maya Singh <m.singh@cedarcrest.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@cedarcrest.com
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:05:00 -0700
Subject: SIG walkthrough and quick pre-call questions

Ben —

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We are kicking off our SIG intake and would like to schedule a 30–45 minute walkthrough to cover your CAIQ responses and any outstanding evidence. Can you share availability for next week (PDT preferred)?

A few quick things we hoped to cover in the pre-call so our engineering team can prepare: 
- Which Redwood environment stores customer data by default (region/data residency)?
- High-level encryption: at-rest and in-transit (algorithms / key management).
- Expected log types you can provide (request logs, model inference logs, audit trails) and retention windows.
- Any SOC 2 reports or attestation dates, and whether you redact customer payloads in logs you share.
- A short list of people you plan to have on the call and their roles.

We need to circulate an internal attendee list and raw evidence request to our infra team, so if you can attach a short evidence inventory that would be helpful.

Thanks — happy to take the call or loop in our infra lead, Liam.

—
Maya Singh
Security Program Manager
Cedarcrest Financial


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Maya Singh <m.singh@cedarcrest.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG walkthrough and quick pre-call questions

Maya — thanks for the note and the list. We can do 30 minutes and propose the following slots (PDT):
- Tue 2026-08-25 09:00–09:30
- Tue 2026-08-25 13:30–14:00
- Wed 2026-08-26 10:00–10:30

Planned Redwood attendees: 
- Ben Carter — Account Executive (ownership/coordination)
- Marissa Cole — Security & Compliance (controls + evidence owner)
- Karthik Iyer — Solutions Engineer (technical deep-dive)

Short answers to your bullets (high level):
- Data residency: hosted Redwood API stores customer request/response data in the region of account creation by default; Dedicated/Private allow explicit region pinning. We will attach a one-page region mapping.
- Encryption: TLS 1.2+ in transit; at-rest AES-256. Customer-managed keys supported via KMS in Private deployments; hosted keys for standard accounts.
- Logs: we can provide request metadata, model inference diagnostics (latency, token counts), system audit logs. Default retention for hosted exports is 90 days; custom retention available for Dedicated/Private.
- SOC 2: Redwood completed SOC 2 Type II in 2025; we can share a redacted attestation under NDA.
- Redaction: for hosted plans we redact PII on a best-effort basis in exported logs; Dedicated/Private give more control over raw payload access.

Attached: REDACTED_CAIQ_Summary.pdf (controls summary), evidence-index.csv (owners + artifact list). We'll flag anything that should be redacted before transit.

If one of those slots works I’ll send a calendar invite with a short agenda.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Maya Singh <m.singh@cedarcrest.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG walkthrough and quick pre-call questions

Hi Maya — grateful for the context. A couple of notes to set expectations ahead of the call so your infra team can plan: 

1) Evidence we typically surface during a SIG: CAIQ matrix, SOC 2 summary (redacted), architecture diagram, log export samples (CSV/JSON), and a mapping of controls to owners. I attached our logging architecture PDF for your SREs to review.
2) For logs: we can provide request-level metadata and operational metrics; we do not provide full customer payloads for hosted accounts without an approved legal/evidence request and redaction steps. Dedicated/Private customers can provide raw logs under their retention policies.
3) Key management details: if you need KMS/HSM specifics, Karthik can walk through the diagram and our KMS integration patterns on the call.

Action items for Cedarcrest before the meeting: 
- Share a short asset list (names of test accounts, sample endpoints) if you want request-level samples.
- Confirm whether you require a redacted SOC 2 report ahead of the call (we require an NDA for full report delivery).

Looking forward to the call — thanks for coordinating.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Head of Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood-logging-arch.pdf (diagram, 2 pages)


From: Maya Singh <m.singh@cedarcrest.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Liam O'Neill <l.oneill@cedarcrest.com>, procurement@cedarcrest.com
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 10:50:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG walkthrough and quick pre-call questions

Thanks Ben / Marissa — the Tue 2026-08-25 13:30–14:00 PDT slot works for our team. Attendees on our side: 
- Maya Singh — Security PM
- Liam O'Neill — Infra Architect (will lead the technical review)
- procurement@cedarcrest.com — procurement lead (on the invite to track evidence requests)

We will circulate a short asset list by EOD Friday and confirm whether we need the redacted SOC 2 in advance (likely yes — procurement prefers it). We are fine with the redaction approach you mentioned; we will share a minimal NDA template if necessary.

Please send the calendar invite and include a dial-in + short agenda (20 minutes controls review, 10 minutes Q&A, 5 minutes next steps). Also, if you can flag any evidence that requires legal sign-off to share, note that in the invite.

—
Maya Singh
Security Program Manager, Cedarcrest Financial

title: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>, Priya Desai <priya@opsly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

Hi Tom / Priya — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap + next steps so we have a common plan.

Call highlights:
- Opsly latency expectations: p95 ~80ms for inference on typical 512-token merchant requests; p99 target currently not required but helpful to track.
- Burst profile: baseline steady-state ~120 QPS (peak hourly 200 QPS), but you saw short spikes up to ~600 QPS for ~20–45s during promotions.
- Security: standard SOC2 controls are required; VPC/private endpoints are a later ask (we discussed Dedicated + Private as options).
- Time-to-value: you want to get an integration live for the holiday pilot in 6 weeks if possible.

Preliminary guidance (what we discussed):
- Hosted API is the fastest path to start (low setup). It's a reasonable fit if your steady-state + short bursts can be handled by autoscaled hosted capacity and you accept a fallback policy for sustained multi-minute spikes. Hosted is great when burst durations are short (<1 minute) and overall p95 target is in the 50–150ms range.
- Dedicated capacity reduces unit cost for high sustained QPS and gives predictable tail performance for strict latency SLOs. It's the recommended path if you see repeated >2 minute spikes at 400–600 QPS or need guaranteed isolation (or want to combine with a Private deployment later).

Concrete next steps I propose: 
1) Opsly: share a 10–30 minute representative traffic trace (sample attached: pilot-qps-sample.csv) — include timestamps and QPS by second plus example request sizes.
2) Redwood: run a short bench against a Redwood-verified model variant (we'll run hosted and a dedicated-style simulation) and return a p50/p95/p99 latency table + throughput curve.
3) Redwood: provide quick cost sketch for a 2-week dedicated pilot (Ben will own) and a hosted fallback pattern to keep the integration live during ramp.
4) Opsly & Redwood: agree on a minimal pilot SLA and a go/no-go checkpoint after the bench.

I've attached a short SLO proposal (slo-proposal.pdf) that maps your p95 target to simple routing rules we can use during the pilot. If you can send the trace by EOD Thursday PST we'll aim to return bench numbers within 48 hours.

Thanks — I’ll coordinate infra for the bench once we have the trace.

—
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference
Attachments: pilot-qps-sample.csv (CSV), slo-proposal.pdf (PDF)

Two quick clarifications: 
- The 600 QPS spikes are usually ~30–40s, but we saw one ~90s spike last quarter.
- Security: our security team will push for a VPC/private option for production, but for the pilot they are willing to accept hosted endpoints with additional audit logging.

Can you also ballpark the difference in monthly cost between a hosted ramp and a small dedicated pool for the pilot so we can flag this to finance? No need for a detailed quote — a rough delta is fine.

Tom
CTO, Opsly

On Tue, Aug 18, 2026 at 3:47 PM Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ava — this is super helpful. I'm attaching a 12-minute trace (high-traffic window from last promo) that includes per-second QPS and payload sizes.
> 
> Two quick clarifications: 
> - The 600 QPS spikes are usually ~30–40s, but we saw one ~90s spike last quarter.

Thanks Tom — that spike pattern is exactly the kind of signal we need.

Ben — can you take point on a quick sketch: 1) estimated monthly delta (hosted vs small dedicated pool for a 2-week pilot), and 2) a short note on whether the 90s spike changes the recommendation (i.e., if a single 90s event is a hard constraint to require Dedicated)?

Neha — I'll loop you on SLO mapping and which telemetry points we should include in the pilot agreement.

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 17:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

Thanks for the context — I've attached a short cost sketch (dedicated-cost-estimate.xlsx) with two scenarios:
- Scenario A (hosted-first): use Hosted API with autoscaling + burst smoothing; expected pilot cost ~ $3.2k for the 2-week window (higher per-token unit).
- Scenario B (dedicated pilot): reserve a small gpool for the pilot with burst headroom; expected pilot cost ~ $7.8k for the 2-week window (lower unit cost, predictable latency).

Observation on the 90s spike: a single 90s event won't automatically force Dedicated, but if you expect repeats (>=3 events/day) or need hard p99 guarantees during promos then Dedicated is the safer option. For the pilot we often recommend starting on Hosted with strict routing (rate limits + a transparent fallback to a slightly lower-capacity model) so you can get live traffic and validate behavior; if bench numbers or security gating require isolation we can flip to a Dedicated pilot quickly.

Next steps from my side: I will confirm the dedicated pilot pricing and include a short appendix that outlines how we handle rapid spikes (auto-burst policies, fallback model variants).

—
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0189

title: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout - urgent
preview:
From: Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-17T09:03:00-07:00
Subject: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout

Hi Lauren,

We're seeing a sharp regression in p95/p99 latency on the Hosted API that's spiking our checkout times and causing payment timeouts. Impact started ~08:52 PDT. Quick numbers from our monitoring: p95 went from ~120ms to ~1.1s, p99 from ~240ms to ~2.8s, error rate jumped from 0.2% to ~2.4% over the last 15 minutes. Throughput for our integration has dipped ~18% as requests back off.

Sample request id: req_9f3b2a (from 2026-08-17T08:54:12Z). We can reproduce on multiple regions (us-west1 primarily). This is customer-facing and blocking checkout flows for ~6% of sessions.

We urgently need a triage and a mitigation plan. Attaching a small snippet of our request logs and a synthetic trace.

Attachments: request_logs_20260817.gz

Thanks,
Alicia Gomez
Head of SRE, Furnishly
Alicia.Gomez@furnishly.com



From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-17T09:07:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout

Hi Alicia — sorry you're seeing this. I'm escalating immediately to SRE and our support squad. Marcus/Jonas/Evelyn: Furnishly reporting a sharp p95/p99 regression starting ~08:52 PDT; sample req id req_9f3b2a attached. Can someone pick this up and confirm scope + mitigation? Will circle back in 30–45 mins with an update.

On Tue, Aug 17, 2026 at 9:03 AM Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com> wrote:
> We're seeing a sharp regression in p95/p99 latency on the Hosted API...

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Account Manager, Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com



From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Cc: Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com>
Date: 2026-08-17T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout — initial triage

Quick triage notes from the runtime team:
- We see a correlated spike in p95/p99 for Furnishly's tenant across the us-west1 pool starting 2026-08-17T08:50:40Z.
- Error-rate increase lines up with several long tail token-generation operations.
- Hypothesis: rapid eviction of prefix/KV cache entries during an autoscale burst caused many cold-key lookups; cold KV hits are adding ~700–1,800ms to tail latency.
- Evidence: KV miss rate for tenant_id=furnishly increased from ~1.8% to ~28% in the 9:00–9:15 window; cold-cache requests show higher kernel time and repeated KV fetch retries.

Immediate actions taken:
1) Rotated Furnishly traffic to a warmed routing plane in us-west1 to reduce cross-pool cold starts (rolled at 09:14 PDT).
2) Triggered an ad-hoc async KV pre-warm job for the hot prefixes observed (started 09:20 PDT).
3) Throttled low-priority background jobs that were contributing to bursty writes to the prefix index.

Request for Furnishly: can you send any recent traffic pattern changes (batch imports, traffic spikes from a new campaign) and confirm the exact request IDs if you have more than the sample.

Attaching host trace we captured: host_trace_20260817.log.gz

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
SRE Manager, Redwood Inference
marcus_lin@redwood.com



From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Cc: Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com>
Date: 2026-08-17T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike — deeper analysis + mitigations

Log excerpt and quick analysis (redacted):
--- begin log snippet ---
2026-08-17T08:54:12.324Z tenant=furnishly req=req_9f3b2a route=hosted-req latency=1824ms kv_hit=false kv_fetch_ms=1396
2026-08-17T08:54:12.328Z tenant=furnishly req=req_9f3b2a retry=1 kv_fetch_ms=1420
--- end log snippet ---

What happened: the routing plane experienced a short concurrent scaling event which caused the placement of several hot prefixes to shift. The automatic placement policy evicted a subset of in-memory prefix entries; when warmed replicas were not immediately available, requests fell back to cold KV which is slower.

Short-term mitigations we recommend (can be enacted in the next 30–120 minutes):
- Enable forced prefix warmup for Furnishly's tenant on their current model/route (we can run this now).
- Temporarily pin Furnishly to the warmed routing plane for 2–6 hours while we run continuous warmers.
- As a safety net, route a small percentage of traffic to a quantized-but-warmed model variant (slightly lower QoE but much faster tail).

Longer term: adjust placement heuristics to avoid evicting hot prefixes during autoscale bursts; add prefix-warmup as part of scale-up lifecycle.

Approval needed from Lauren/Evelyn to: 1) pin routing plane and 2) allow a short-term increase in reserved capacity (this may incur credits/cost). Once approved we'll enact the routing change and confirm with Furnishly.

—Jonas Weber
Support Lead, Redwood Inference
jonas_weber@redwood.com



From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-17T10:18:00-07:00
Subject: Exec alert: Furnishly outage impact + recommended mitigations (requesting approval)

Evelyn — Furnishly (important customer, checkout impact) is experiencing a p95/p99 regression caused by prefix cache evictions during a scale event. Marcus and Jonas have implemented temporary mitigations (warmed routing and pre-warm jobs) and recommend:
- Pin to warmed routing plane for 4 hours
- Run continuous prefix warmers for the next 8 hours
- Temporarily increase reserved capacity for Furnishly (estimate ~$1,800 for the next 24 hours)

Requesting your approval to apply the pinned routing + capacity bump now. If approved I'll communicate the mitigation plan and timeline to Alicia; we should also offer Furnishly a goodwill credit for the disruption (TBD).

On the customer side Alicia has accepted our workaround but asks for an RCA and timeline for a permanent fix.

Lauren Bishop
Account Manager, Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com



From: Alicia Gomez <agomez@furnishly.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-18T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout — ack and next steps

Thanks for the fast response and the temporary mitigation. We saw tail latency return to expected ranges after the pin/warm job (p95 back to ~140ms, p99 ~260ms). Furnishly Product wants a short RCA and a timeline for the placement/eviction fix and any permanent guardrails you plan to add.

Please also document the goodwill credit and a short statement on what monitoring/alerts will prevent this without manual pinning in the future. We can coordinate a post-mortem call tomorrow morning if needed.

Attachments: none

Alicia
Head of SRE, Furnishly
agomez@furnishly.com

Subject: URGENT: Production 5xx/429 spike + retry storm impacting checkout

Aditya —

We observed a sustained elevation in 5xx and 429 responses across our checkout and patient-intake endpoints starting ~2026-08-16T23:45Z. From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Li <ops@solarahealth.com>, Raj Patel <cto@solarahealth.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Aug 2026 09:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: URGENT: Production 5xx/429 spike + retry storm impacting checkout

Aditya / team —

Quick triage update from SRE: we saw a cascading failure triggered by an autoscaler policy change deployed accidentally in the EU region that incorrectly classified long-tail requests as cold and scaled down inference workers while traffic spiked from Solara. Client-side retries (no jitter) amplified traffic and saturated the ingress queue, leading to 429s and downstream 5xx as requests timed out waiting for workers.

Mitigation applied (completed 09:30 PDT):
- Reverted autoscaler policy to previous stable config (rollback complete)
- Temporarily applied conservative rate limit for Solara's IP range to reduce retry amplification
- Forced warm KV cache for affected model family and pre-warmed two additional inference pools in us-west-2
- Enabled a circuit-breaker to shed excess requests and return fast failures to avoid queue pileups

Current status: error rate falling; we estimate 90% of affected requests have recovered; working on full catch-up for delayed requests. We will produce a detailed RCA and timeline in the next 48 hours and share mitigation artifacts.

Attachment: initial-mitigation-timeline.md (internal)

Hannah Schmitt
SRE Manager — Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference
