title: Capacity wiring playbook — MAP staging & acceptance checkpoints
preview:
From: Claire Donovan <c.donovan@mediushealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procure@mediushealth.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Initial MAP questions — staging window and capacity needs

Thanks for the intro, Ben. We finished the internal scoping and want to make sure our MAP covers the capacity gating items. Two quick asks: 1) realistic lead time if we opt for Private (on-prem/VPC) with H100s, and 2) if Dedicated is acceptable, what minimum pool size you recommend to support a pilot of ~50 concurrent sessions (avg 512 tokens) plus a small evaluation batch workload.

Claire
Head of ML Ops, Medius Health

---
Attachment: requirements-summary.csv (stub)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Donovan <c.donovan@mediushealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Initial MAP questions — staging window and capacity needs

Claire — thanks, this is inline with what we expected. High-level answers up front, then a proposed MAP stub sent as an attachment for sign-off.

- Private (on-prem/VPC) with customer-provided H100s: procurement + validation + networking + KMS integration normally takes 10–14 weeks after hardware delivery; hardware lead time varies but plan for 12–16 weeks to source H100s today.
- Redwood Private (we host in your VPC with our private control plane): ~8–10 weeks for onboarding and acceptance assuming SOC2 evidence in flight and PKI/KMS access ready.
- Dedicated (Redwood-managed reserved capacity): fastest path — we can provision a Dedicated pool in ~4–6 weeks for A100-equivalent instances depending on region and tranche availability.

Sizing recommendation for pilot (50 concurrent sessions, avg 512 tokens per session): baseline 32 A100-equivalent units to meet latency SLO ~150–250ms per token with continuous batching; hedge scenario 48 units if you expect bursty inference or long-context experiments. If you have a heavy embedding/rerank job during eval, add a transient batch node group (cost-efficient).

Attached: MAP-draft-v3.xlsx — I sketched milestones, acceptance criteria, and contingency lanes (hedge and fallback to smaller quantized model). Key MAP checkpoints:
  - Eval kickoff: infra provisioned for staging (dev-only subset)
  - Validation window: perf and regression tests (30% traffic synthetic)
  - Security gating: SOC2 evidence intake + remediation window
  - Procurement handoff: invoicing and PO window
  - Go/no-go and MSA signature

Please review the staging acceptance criteria on tab "Staging-Accept" and confirm whether Medius needs tighter MLPerf-style benchmarks or if the perf/regression suite described is sufficient.

Ben
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

Attachments: MAP-draft-v3.xlsx, staging-acceptance-checklist.md


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Claire Donovan <c.donovan@mediushealth.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.ai>, Procurement Team <procure@mediushealth.com>
Date: Mon, 17 Aug 2026 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Capacity wiring playbook — MAP staging & acceptance checkpoints

Adding a short ops-focused checklist from the Dedicated/onboarding perspective. Priyom and I aligned on these items that need owners inside Medius to hit the 4–6 week Dedicated timeline:

- Region confirmation & IP allowlist (owner: Medius network) — 3 business days to provide
- KMS access & key rotation policy (owner: Medius security) — required for Private; optional for Dedicated if Redwood KMS is used
- Test traffic generator + canonical prompt set (owner: Medius ML) — needed for perf/regress tests by day 3 of staging
- SOC2 artifact dropbox link (owner: Medius compliance) — we can accept partial evidence and continue onboarding in parallel, but final acceptance requires SOC2 checklist satisfied

Contingency lanes we recommend:
  - Fast lane (Dedicated): commit to a 12-week pacing with a 2-week buffer; rollback to quantized q4xx model if perf issues
  - Hedge lane (Private): start procurement immediately; schedule a parallel Dedicated tranching to avoid schedule risk

If Medius wants a firm procurement schedule for H100s we can put you in touch with our procurement partner who manages hardware orders with a target lead time of 12 weeks minimum.

Marissa
Director, Customer Onboarding | Redwood Inference


From: Claire Donovan <c.donovan@mediushealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procure@mediushealth.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 10:06:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Capacity wiring playbook — MAP staging & acceptance checkpoints

Appreciate the detail — this helps a lot. A few clarifications from our side before we align on the MAP:

1) Latency SLO: 200ms median token is tight for our product; we need clear degradation behavior documented if tail latency rises during peak. Can you add an acceptance metric that ties degraded P95 to a fallback model?
2) SOC2: we can provide initial evidence within 10 business days; final attestation will take longer. Do you treat that as a hard blocker for Private go-live?
3) Procurement: our budget cycle closes in 6 weeks. If we want Private, we need a realistic signed MSA + PO path inside that window. Can Redwood offer a short-term Dedicated bridge if hardware delivery slips?

I'll loop in Finance next. If the answers are green, we can move to a formal MAP sign-off.

Claire

---
Attachment: compliance-timeline.xlsx (stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Donovan <c.donovan@mediushealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.ai>, Procurement Team <procure@mediushealth.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Capacity wiring playbook — MAP staging & acceptance checkpoints

Claire — great questions. Short answers and a proposed change to the MAP that we will circulate as v4: 

1) Latency SLO & fallback: we will add a P95/P99 tier to the acceptance matrix. If P95 > 500ms during the validation window, the MAP triggers an automated fallback to a 2-stage plan: (a) switch traffic to a smaller quantized model variant for non-critical endpoints, (b) activate an extra Dedicated burst tranche to bring P95 under threshold. We'll document exact thresholds in MAP v4.

2) SOC2 gating: for Private go-live we require SOC2 artifacts to be in our evidence intake and a remediation plan agreed; we can allow Dedicated onboarding to proceed with SOC2 evidence in-flight, but final Private production cutover is contingent on SOC2 completion. We will mark that as a hard gate in v4 but allow parallel work to minimize schedule impact.

3) Procurement & bridge: yes — we recommend a formal MAP lane that commits to Dedicated (12-week reserved tranche) as the primary path, with Private procurement running in parallel as hedge. If hardware delivery slips, the Dedicated bridge can cover production needs until Private is ready; we'll include cost accounting for a 60-day Dedicated bridge in the MAP appendix.

Next steps I propose: Priyom to update the MAP workbook with the P95/P99 acceptance table and the Dedicated-bridge cost appendix; Marissa will draft the owner/responsibility table for each checkpoint; Claire/Procurement to confirm PO windows and an internal owner for KMS. Can we aim to recirculate MAP-v4 by Friday 21 Aug?

Ben
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

---

title: Customer-led data boundary commitments for Private VPCs
preview:
From: Elena Morales <elena.morales@stratusbank.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-17T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Customer-led data boundary commitments for Private VPCs

Hi Ben / Evelyn,

Thanks for the demo last week — Stratus is getting closer to a procurement OK. Legal has one outstanding set of questions we need to close before we can finish the security review:

1) For a Private (VPC) deployment where we provide the AWS account and S3 buckets, can Redwood contractually commit that all customer-controlled storage (S3, EBS, EFS snapshots) will remain physically resident in our AWS us-east-1 account/region and that no copies will be retained outside of it?

2) What is Redwood's deletion process for any artifacts you might hold (logs, support bundles, backup copies), and can you provide a time-bound attestation/certificate once a deletion is executed?

3) We also need example contract language and a short mutual action plan (timeline + deliverables) so Legal can include it in the addendum.

Would you be able to send sample clause text and an example of the deletion attestation? Ideally by EOD tomorrow — the procurement timeline is tight.

Thanks,
Elena Morales
Director, Information Security
Stratus Bank
elena.morales@stratusbank.com

---
Note: attaching detailed infrastructure diagram from the demo would help; we can accept redacted diagrams if needed.

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@stratusbank.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-17T11:00:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Customer-led data boundary commitments for Private VPCs
Attachments: vpc-data-bindings.pdf (application/pdf); soc2-snapshot-2026-06.pdf (application/pdf)

Elena — thanks for the clear asks and the timeline. High level answers below and I attached a short draft clause + a SOC 2 snapshot you can share with Legal.

1) Residency: For Private VPC deployments where the customer owns the cloud account and storage, Redwood's data plane runs inside the customer's account (we mount or write to customer-provided buckets/volumes). In that configuration Redwood does not create secondary copies in our hosted storage. The only exceptions are:
  - Temporary in-memory caches on our managed nodes while processing requests (TTL < 30 minutes by default).
  - Support bundles and diagnostic traces that may be uploaded to our support workspace if you explicitly opt-in during a troubleshooting session.

We can include contractual language that commits to regional-bound writes and prohibits cross-region replication outside us-east-1 for customer data.

2) Deletion and attestation: We operate two deletion paths depending on artifact type:
  - Customer-owned storage (S3/EBS): deletion is executed in your account; we provide a deletion manifest (S3 delete markers, timestamps, keys) and a signed job id for traceability.
  - Control-plane artifacts (audit logs, support bundles): we can run a one-off certified wipe and provide an attestation PDF that includes the job id, start/end timestamps, hashes of deleted archives, and an operator sign-off.

3) Sample language / map: see attached vpc-data-bindings.pdf (draft clause + mutual action plan). Suggested timeline: 5 business days to agree clause, 10 business days to schedule the initial deletion attestation after contract signature.

I'm looping in Karthik (security) to confirm the attestation contents and whether we should require customer-held KMS keys for additional guarantees.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.ai

---
Attachment stubs: vpc-data-bindings.pdf, soc2-snapshot-2026-06.pdf

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elena Morales <elena.morales@stratusbank.com>
Date: 2026-08-18T08:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Customer-led data boundary commitments for Private VPCs

Adding a few security details / suggested contract text for Legal. Keep this high level in the contract and push operational detail into an SOW / runbook appendix.

- Ownership & residency clause (suggested): "Customer data stored in resources provisioned within Customer's cloud account will not be copied, transferred, or backed-up to Redwood-managed storage outside of Customer's specified region (us-east-1) except for transient in-memory processing and explicit troubleshooting uploads initiated by Customer or with Customer's documented consent."

- Deletion attestation (operational flow):
  1) Customer requests deletion via ticket referencing contract clause and provides resource identifiers.
  2) Redwood executes deletion in Customer's account (for customer-owned S3/EBS) and generates a deletion manifest (object keys, delete marker timestamps, job-id).
  3) For control-plane artifacts (support bundles/diagnostics), Redwood runs a cryptographically verifiable wipe and produces a signed attestation PDF that includes: job-id, operator id, start/end times, SHA256 hashes of deleted archives, and the S3 object keys (if applicable).
  4) Redwood retains an audit record of the attestation request and the signed attestation for 12 months for compliance evidence (we can adjust this retention to match your needs).

- KMS/Key control: Recommend driver language to require customer-managed CMKs for encryption-at-rest, and that Redwood will not hold unencrypted backups or key material. This gives the strongest technical guarantee.

- Backups & snapshots: Add an explicit carve-out that scheduled backups/snapshots within the customer's account are under the customer's retention policy and deletion requests will coordinate with backup/restore windows to avoid inadvertent data loss; Redwood will document timeline impacts in the mutual action plan.

If Legal wants, I can provide a one-page attestation template they can use to accept delivered evidence. Ben / Evelyn — do we want to send the template directly or route through Legal first?

Karthik Iyer
Head of Security — Redwood Inference
karthik_iyer@redwood.ai

---

> Elena — thanks for the clear asks and the timeline. High level answers below and I attached a short draft clause + a SOC 2 snapshot you can share with Legal.

title: Action required: complete supplier activation for Lumen Fabrications
preview:
From: Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 09:37:00 -0700
Subject: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi Ava,

We tried to complete the supplier activation link you sent this morning but the button on the activation page doesn't appear on our mobile webview (Android Chrome). Tapping the blank area does nothing. I attached a screenshot.

We can complete from desktop for now, but our AP team mostly works from tablets while on site and this will block our PO acknowledgements later this week. Can you resend a link or suggest a workaround?

Thanks,
Thomas Rey
Head of AP, Lumen Fabrications
onboarding@lumenfab.com
Attachment: screenshot-cta-hidden.png (image/png, 248 KB)


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 10:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi Thomas — thanks for the heads up and the screenshot.

Short answer: we saw a small subset of activation links render an empty CTA on certain Android WebViews when the deep-link parameter string exceeded ~1200 characters. That happens when a long return_url + campaign tracking + marketplace params are included. We pushed a mitigation last week but it looks like your link still had a long param set.

Workarounds:
- Open the link in a desktop browser (what you noted)
- Long-term: I'll reissue a trimmed activation link (no campaign params) and paste the direct deep-link token in the email body so mobile clients don't drop the button.

I'll re-send now and loop in Ben for an ops follow-up to ensure our link generator strips non-essential tracking for supplier activation flows.

Ava Chen
Enterprise Onboarding (Redwood Inference)
--
On Sat, Aug 15, 2026 at 09:37 AM Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com> wrote:
> Hi Ava,
> 
> We tried to complete the supplier activation link you sent this morning but the button on the activation page doesn't appear on our mobile webview (Android Chrome).
> 
> Thanks,
> Thomas


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Cc: Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 08:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi team,

I dug into the generator and confirmed the issue: the link builder appended both hb_campaign_id and ga_client_id for marketing attribution. On small-screen WebViews the layout engine treated the long querystring as part of the inline button rendering, causing the CTA to flow off-canvas.

Planned fixes:
1) Short-term patch to strip non-required query params for activation emails (deploy tomorrow morning).
2) Telemetry: add a hit to capture activation page client type and render errors so we can proactively detect regressions.

Ava — can you resend the trimmed activation link to Thomas now? I'll open a ticket (SUP-8123) to track the patch and include a regression test against Android embedded WebView flows.

Ben
Customer Ops
Attachment: onboarding-instructions.pdf (application/pdf, 112 KB)

Activation link: https://redwood.app/activate?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9--shorttoken123

- We've filed SUP-8123 to make the fix permanent. Priya, can you add the Android WebView regression to the automation backlog?

Thanks — let me know when you're activated and I'll confirm PO visibility from our side.

Best,

title: Next steps: EdgeGate private deployment onboarding & egress validation
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Samir Shah <samir@edgegate.io>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya.rao@edgegate.io>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Next steps: EdgeGate private deployment onboarding & egress validation

Hi Samir / Priya —

Great conversation earlier; thanks for walking us through EdgeGate's compliance constraints. Per the call, it sounds like you are trending toward a Private VPC deployment (Redwood Private) because of the data residency and audit requirements. To keep momentum, here are the concrete items we talked about and a proposed owner for each: 

- Confirm PrivateLink vs VPC Peering decision (EdgeGate network team) — you mentioned PrivateLink is preferred, but wanted final sign-off.
- Firewall / egress IP allowlist (EdgeGate ops) — we need the list of CIDRs that will be allowed outbound to Redwood (or confirmation you will allow our egress IP ranges).
- Ports & protocols (Redwood / EdgeGate) — quick matrix so NetOps can pre-stage firewall rules.
- KMS & key custody preference (EdgeGate security) — whether you'll use BYOK or Redwood-managed keys.
- Timeline & procurement (Samir) — target date to cutover staging traffic.

I've attached a short onboarding checklist and a CSV template to capture egress IPs + port rules. Ben Carter (cc'd) from Solutions will schedule a 30-min follow-up with your network team to validate the flow.

Next step I propose: Ben + Karthik run the network checklist workshop on Wed 8/19 at 10:00 PT. If that works, please confirm attendees and whether you'll share a read-only VPC diagram ahead of the call.

Thanks,
Camila Reyes
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0193

Attachments: redwood_private_deploy_checklist.pdf, egress_ip_matrix.csv


From: Samir Shah <samir@edgegate.io>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya.rao@edgegate.io>, Liam Turner <liam.turner@edgegate-ops.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 16:01:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Next steps: EdgeGate private deployment onboarding & egress validation

Thanks Camila — this is helpful. Quick clarifications from our side: 

- We prefer PrivateLink for the initial rollout since our regulators want no cross-tenant routing through vendor VPCs. We'll still want to validate latency and egress inspection flows but PrivateLink is the favored path.
- For egress, we need your full list of outbound IP ranges (source IPs that Redwood will use when contacting our systems) and also the destination IPs Redwood expects to call back into EdgeGate (if any).
- Ports: we generally only open 443 outbound, but our IDS team may request to mirror traffic for a short validation window.
- KMS: BYOK is preferred — security will share our key rotation policy.

I'll loop in Liam (Network Ops) to coordinate the workshop. Please ensure Ben has the port matrix filled out so Liam can assess policy impact before the call.

Samir Shah
Director of Platform Engineering | EdgeGate
samir@edgegate.io

On Sun, Aug 16, 2026 at 15:12 Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com> wrote:
> (original message quoted)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Samir Shah <samir@edgegate.io>, Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 16:38:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Next steps: EdgeGate private deployment onboarding & egress validation

Hi Samir —

Thanks for the clarity. A few nitty-gritty items Ben from Solutions here (I'll be the primary for the workshop):

1) Egress IPs: Camila attached an "egress_ip_matrix.csv" template. Ideally you can fill source CIDRs (if you control NAT egress) or tell us to publish a static set of Redwood egress addresses we can use. For PrivateLink, the main ask is which of your subnets will host the endpoint and whether those subnets have internet egress enabled.

2) PrivateLink vs Peering: since you prefer PrivateLink, we'll prepare a short latency/throughput checklist and a fallback peering plan if we hit throughput constraints.

3) Ports/health checks: we will need to open outbound 443 and allow health-check traffic from our managed probes (we'll list the probe IPs once the staging endpoint is up). Also: TCP 22 is optional only if you want our ops to do in-band troubleshooting (usually not needed).

4) Certificates & TLS termination: confirm whether EdgeGate will terminate TLS at the PrivateLink endpoint or Redwood should present certs to the endpoint.

Action items for the workshop (we'll take notes and produce a connectivity runbook):
- EdgeGate: populate egress_ip_matrix.csv + attach a simple VPC/subnet diagram (PNG/PDF).
- Redwood: produce an endpoint sizing recommendation and egress IP list for staging.

Propose Wed 8/19 10:00 PT for a 30-min workshop. If that works, I'll send a calendar invite and include a short agenda + the runbook template.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

Attachments: privatelink-vs-peering-summary.pdf


From: Liam Turner <liam.turner@edgegate-ops.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Shah <samir@edgegate.io>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 17:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Next steps: EdgeGate private deployment onboarding & egress validation

Ben / Camila —

Liam from EdgeGate NetOps. Wed 8/19 10:00 PT works for our team. For the workshop we will bring: 
- VPC diagram (will send prior to the meeting)
- Current egress CIDR blocks list (I will fill the CSV)
- Firewall policy owner (we will want a pre-approved contact)

One policy note: our security team requires a 72-hour validation window where IDS logs are mirrored to our audit tenant before we flip production traffic. Please include any short-term logging endpoints Redwood needs to enable.

I'll upload the VPC diagram and the CSV into the shared HubSpot record tonight.

Subject: Integration: PromptRunners connector for LangChain & LlamaIndex

Hi Ben,

Quick intro — I’m Alex, Head of Integrations at PromptRunners. We built a lightweight connector that plugs into LangChain and LlamaIndex runtimes to add client-side prompt observability and distributed prompt templates. A few customers asked if we could be Redwood-verified so they can deploy on Redwood Dedicated/Private with confidence.

Ben

title: Commit tiers and guardrails — per-token pricing / price protections (ACME)
preview:
From: Jill Martinez <jill.martinez@acmecorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Simon Blake <simon.blake@acmecorp.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Commit tiers and guardrails — per-token pricing / price protections (ACME)

Thanks for the demo today, Ben. We like Redwood's latency profile and the ability to pin model versions. Before we move to pricing approvals I need clarity on a couple of protection items ACME legal/finance care about:

- Can you commit to a 12-month price lock on per-token rates for committed tiers?
- Does Redwood offer an MFN clause (i.e., will ACME be protected if you later publish a lower per-token rate to another customer)?
- We have an internal policy that annual increases tied to inflation must be capped; are you able to support a CPI cap (we typically see requests for a 3% cap).

Also attaching the procurement questionnaire — can you confirm which of these are acceptable for an enterprise addendum?

Best,
Jill
Director, Vendor Management
ACME Corporation

---

On Mon, 10 Aug 2026 at 08:02, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
> To: Jill Martinez <jill.martinez@acmecorp.com>
> Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 08:02:00 -0700
> Subject: Commit tier options & simple protections — summary and next steps
>
> Hi Jill — thanks for your time earlier. Per our conversation I put together three commit tiers (monthly committed spend) with example effective per-token rates at typical usage profiles. Key points:
>
> - We can offer 6, 12, and 24 month commit options. Longer commits yield deeper per-token discounts and eligible for a 12-month or 24-month price lock depending on the term.
> - MFN: Redwood generally provides parity on published publicly-available committed offers; true one-to-one MFN requires an explicit clause — we can negotiate limited MFN scope (e.g., published committed SKU parity for comparable volumes and regions).
> - CPI cap: we typically allow modest annual adjustments; standard cap we propose is CPI + 0% with an absolute ceiling (we can do an absolute cap of 3% per year for enterprise contracts).
>
> I'll need to loop in Marissa (revenue lead) and Stephanie (legal) to confirm the exact language for MFN and price-lock trigger conditions.
>
> Attached: redwood-pricing-summary.xlsx (example tiers), redwood-term-highlights.docx
>
> Next step: if the above is directionally OK, I can send a short term-sheet and proposed addendum for your procurement/legal to review.
>
> Cheers,
>Ben
>Account Executive, Redwood Inference
>
---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Aug 2026 10:23:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Commit tier options & simple protections — ACME asks on price lock / MFN / CPI cap

Folks — forwarding ACME's questions (below). They want 12-month price lock, MFN, and CPI cap guidance. My suggested negotiation posture is below:

> Suggested posture (for internal alignment):
> - Price lock: commit to a 12-month price lock for committed spend tiers so long as ACME maintains the agreed committed capacity each month (we define monthly committed units and a 60% utilization threshold to avoid overcommit exposure).
> - MFN: offer a limited MFN that covers publicly-published committed SKUs for equivalent volumes/regions; carve out one-off promotional deals and private strategic discounts (we cannot broadly guarantee every bespoke discount).
> - CPI cap: agree to an annual CPI-based adjustment with an absolute ceiling of 3% per year; if CPI goes above that, Redwood will absorb the difference.

Can you two draft quick language for the term sheet that captures the above? Also flag any red lines.

Thanks,
Ben

---

From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Aug 2026 14:50:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Commit tier options & simple protections — ACME asks on price lock / MFN / CPI cap

Ben — quick draft language below you can send to ACME. Marissa, flagging commercial exposure but this is common.

> 1) Price Lock. Redwood will guarantee the per-token rates set forth in the attached term sheet for a period of twelve (12) months from the Effective Date, provided Customer meets the monthly committed spend levels as defined in Schedule A. If Customer fails to meet the committed spend level for two consecutive months, Redwood may re-evaluate pricing with thirty (30) days' notice.
>
> 2) MFN. Redwood will provide Most-Favored-Nation treatment with respect to publicly-published committed pricing SKUs for enterprise customers of comparable size and region. This MFN specifically excludes: (a) short-term promotional pricing; (b) bespoke strategic arrangements documented under separate master agreements; and (c) discounts tied to co-sell or marketplace rebates. Redwood reserves the right to demonstrate comparability in good faith.
>
> 3) CPI Cap. Annual price adjustments (if any) shall be tied to the US Consumer Price Index (CPI-U) but in no event shall adjustments exceed three percent (3%) in any twelve (12) month period.

I also recommend (i) a definition of "comparable size/region" in Schedule B and (ii) a soft-exit protection: if Redwood publishes materially lower baseline public pricing for the same committed SKU during the lock period, Redwood will apply the lower pricing to Customer for the remainder of the lock period (subject to the exclusions in MFN).

Marissa — commercial impact: if we allow full retroactive MFN we can see revenue leakage on high-velocity deals. Proposed limited MFN + published-SKU parity reduces risk. Thoughts?

-Stephanie

---

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Aug 2026 09:07:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Commit tier options & simple protections — ACME asks on price lock / MFN / CPI cap

Agree with Stephanie's wording. From a commercial perspective:

- Push for definition of "monthly committed units" and include a make-good: if ACME misses a month due to seasonal drops we can allow a one-time make-up window within the quarter.
- For MFN, require ACME to notify us and provide evidence of published pricing they believe is better; we will validate comparability within 15 business days.
- CPI cap at 3% is fine; if CPI spikes programmatically (e.g., >6%) we need an executive review clause.

I can approve the limited MFN / 12-month lock language for AE to send as a term-sheet. Ben — go ahead and share the attached short term-sheet with ACME and request procurement contact for final doc routing.

Marissa
Head of Revenue, Redwood Inference

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jill Martinez <jill.martinez@acmecorp.com>, Simon Blake <simon.blake@acmecorp.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 13 Aug 2026 11:30:00 -0700
Subject: Proposed term-sheet — commit tiers, price lock & limited MFN (attached)

Jill — please find a short term-sheet attached (acme-commit-term-sheet.pdf) capturing the positions we discussed: 12-month price lock, limited MFN for published committed SKUs, CPI cap at 3%, make-good window for one missed month per quarter, and definitions for monthly committed units.

High-level example (rounded):
- 6 month commit: $0.09 / 1k input tokens effective at 25M tokens/month
- 12 month commit: $0.07 / 1k input tokens effective at 50M tokens/month (12-month price lock)
- 24 month commit: $0.055 / 1k input tokens effective at 75M tokens/month (24-month price lock)

Note: public host SKU parity MFN limited to equivalent committed tiers and excluding promos/bespoke deals. Please review and let me know procurement contact and any redlines. Happy to hop on a legal call to go through the MFN carveouts.

Regards,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachment: acme-commit-term-sheet.pdf

---

From: Simon Blake <simon.blake@acmecorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jill Martinez <jill.martinez@acmecorp.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 10:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Proposed term-sheet — commit tiers, price lock & limited MFN (attached)

Ben — thanks. A few follow-ups from procurement/legal:

- ACME requests the MFN to include retroactive application to our effective date if Redwood publishes a lower committed-SKU public rate within the 12-month lock. Is Redwood willing to grandfather ACME to the lower rate for the remainder of the lock period?
- We also request an explicit 12-month price lock (no re-evaluation) unless ACME materially fails to meet committed thresholds. Your earlier language about re-evaluation after 2 consecutive months of shortfall is acceptable if accompanied by a one-time make-good window and clear measurement method.
- On CPI cap: 3% cap is acceptable, but we request that if CPI exceeds 3% Redwood will engage in good-faith discussions and not unilaterally increase pricing beyond published caps during the term.

Can you get legal to sign off on the retroactive grandfathering ask?

Simon
VP Product Ops, ACME

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Simon Blake <simon.blake@acmecorp.com>, Jill Martinez <jill.martinez@acmecorp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2026 17:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Proposed term-sheet — commit tiers, price lock & limited MFN (attached)

Simon / Jill — quick update after internal review. We can accept retroactive grandfathering to the lower published committed-SKU rate for the remainder of the lock period, subject to: (i) the lower rate must be a publicly-published committed SKU (not a bespoke strategic arrangement), and (ii) ACME must notify Redwood in writing within 30 days of public publication and provide proof of the published SKU. Redwood will validate comparability within 15 business days and, if confirmed, apply the lower rate prospectively for the remainder of ACME's lock period (no retroactive credits).

We will also explicitly include the one-time make-good window (within the same quarter) for missed committed units and keep the CPI cap at 3% with an executive review clause if CPI > 6%. Stephanie and Marissa are on the thread for signature routing and final redlines.

If that sounds OK I can have a DocuSign-ready addendum routed — please share the procurement contact and desired billing cadence.

Thanks — Ben

Folks — forwarding ACME's questions (below). They want 12-month price lock, MFN, and CPI cap guidance. My suggested negotiation posture is below:

> Suggested posture (for internal alignment):
> - Price lock: commit to a 12-month price lock for committed spend tiers so long as ACME maintains the agreed committed capacity each month (we define monthly committed units and a 60% utilization threshold to avoid overcommit exposure).
> - MFN: offer a limited MFN that covers publicly-published committed SKUs for equivalent volumes/regions; carve out one-off promotional deals and private strategic discounts (we cannot broadly guarantee every bespoke discount).
> - CPI cap: agree to an annual CPI-based adjustment with an absolute ceiling of 3% per year; if CPI goes above that, Redwood will absorb the difference.

From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Aug 2026 14:50:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Commit tier options & simple protections — ACME asks on price lock / MFN / CPI cap

Ben — quick draft language below you can send to ACME. Marissa, flagging commercial exposure but this is common.

> 1) Price Lock. Redwood will guarantee the per-token rates set forth in the attached term sheet for a period of twelve (12) months from the Effective Date, provided Customer meets the monthly committed spend levels as defined in Schedule A. If Customer fails to meet the committed spend level for two consecutive months, Redwood may re-evaluate pricing with thirty (30) days' notice.
>
> 2) MFN. Redwood will provide Most-Favored-Nation treatment with respect to publicly-published committed pricing SKUs for enterprise customers of comparable size and region. This MFN specifically excludes: (a) short-term promotional pricing; (b) bespoke strategic arrangements documented under separate master agreements; and (c) discounts tied to co-sell or marketplace rebates. Redwood reserves the right to demonstrate comparability in good faith.
>
> 3) CPI Cap. Annual price adjustments (if any) shall be tied to the US Consumer Price Index (CPI-U) but in no event shall adjustments exceed three percent (3%) in any twelve (12) month period.

I also recommend (i) a definition of "comparable size/region" in Schedule B and (ii) a soft-exit protection: if Redwood publishes materially lower baseline public pricing for the same committed SKU during the lock period, Redwood will apply the lower pricing to Customer for the remainder of the lock period (subject to the exclusions in MFN).

Marissa — commercial impact: if we allow full retroactive MFN we can see revenue leakage on high-velocity deals. Proposed limited MFN + published-SKU parity reduces risk. Thoughts?

-Stephanie

title: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T09:12:00-04:00
Subject: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Hi Ellen —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. As we talked through on the demo, the primary commercial question you're raising is whether Redwood can include a steady-state throughput assurance for the reserved pool that backs your inference service (THL-Prod). Specifically: a commitment around sustained tokens/sec for steady workloads and explicit headroom rules for preemption/eviction during multi-tenant bursts.

I've sketched a conservative SLO amendment that:
- Guarantees 95th-percentile steady throughput at a committed concurrency profile (attached).
- Defines preemption windows and a 72-hr notice for planned maintenance that impacts reserved capacity.
- Specifies fallback routing (regional failover + lower-precision quantized model variants) in the event of capacity shortfalls.

Can you confirm whether the key ask from procurement is: (a) a numeric sustained throughput figure + credits for missed windows, or (b) a qualitative commitment (e.g., \"reasonable efforts + escalation playbook\")? We can draft either — the former requires slightly wider headroom planning on our side.

I've cc'd Laura (customer success) and Aditya (sales engineering) so they can align on the operational playbook if you want to proceed with a redline. If useful, I can attach a short throughput‑modeling workbook for your infra team to validate assumptions.

Next steps: if procurement wants a numeric SLA, we propose we send a formal amendment + the throughput model and run a quick joint validation with your infra (30–45 mins).

Thanks —

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
Attachment stubs: thalassa_sla_amendment_v1.pdf (draft), throughput_modeling_v1.xlsx (model)


From: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@thalassa.io, Marco Ruiz <m.ruiz@thalassa.io>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T11:05:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Ben —

Appreciate the quick follow-up and the draft. A few clarifying questions from our side before we bring legal in:

1) Numeric target — when you say "95th-percentile steady throughput at a committed concurrency profile", can you give an example figure and the concurrency assumptions? We need numbers to map onto our cost model.
2) Credits — how are missed windows measured? Rolling 24h windows or discrete outage events?
3) Isolation — will our reserved pool be fully isolated for scheduled capacity (no noisy neighbors), or is it a soft reservation with priority?
4) Fallback behavior — what is the expected latency/quality delta if you route to a quantized variant? We need an SLA that preserves core product quality for our end-users.

If you can answer these at a high level we can decide whether procurement takes the draft to legal or if we route to infra for validation first.

— Ellen Park
Head of Platform Ops | Thalassa Analytics
ellen.park@thalassa.io


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-13T08:30:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Ellen — thanks, good questions. Quick answers below and attached a slightly expanded amendment + the modeling workbook so your infra/procurement can poke at numbers: thalassa_sla_amendment_v1.pdf, throughput_modeling_v1.xlsx.

1) Numeric example: for a committed concurrency of 40 steady sessions (avg token generation 40 tok/sec/session with 1.5x burst headroom), the model projects a sustainable nominal throughput of ~2,400 tok/sec at the 95th percentile. We can treat this as a template — if you want 60 sessions we update the numbers accordingly. The workbook includes a toggled table.

2) Measurement: we propose discrete missed-window measurements measured against 1-hour evaluation buckets across the billing day. A missed window is when sustained throughput for that 1-hour bucket falls below 85% of the committed nominal for >10 minutes (cumulative). Credits are pro-rated per missed bucket. If you'd prefer rolling 24h windows, we can update language.

3) Isolation: reserved pool == soft isolation with priority reservation. Practically: your workloads run on reserved nodes with preemption protection for certain throttles; however we reserve a small shared emergency buffer for platform health events. The amendment defines preemption tiers and a guaranteed minimum of 70% of the committed steady throughput even during prioritized preemption events (subject to scheduled maintenance windows).

4) Fallback latency/quality: routing to the quantized variant will typically add 10–30ms of tail latency on inference and may reduce token-level perplexity slightly for long-form generation. We call out an "acceptable quality window" in the amendment: business-critical prompts (flagged via API metadata) will not be routed to lower-precision variants unless explicitly authorized in the runbook.

If those points look reasonable, propose we run a 30–45 minute infra validation session (aditya + laura + your infra contact) to align on the numbers and then move the amendment to procurement/legal. When would be a good time for Thalassa?

Best,

Ben
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-13T14:20:00-04:00
Subject: Fwd: Steady-state throughput assurance — we need a tech sanity check

Forwarding Ben's reply to loop you in — Thalassa liked the shape of our answer but procurement will want the numbers validated. David, can you confirm two things for the modeling team:

- The 70% minimum during prioritized preemption events is conservative enough given current preemption mechanics on r8-reserved nodes (NVIDIA). Any recommended language changes?
- The emergency buffer sizing: the workbook assumes a 15% platform buffer shared across customers for short system windows; is 15% safe or should we use 20% for conservative quoting?

I'll put the workbook in the shared drive after you confirm; we want to avoid back-and-forth with procurement. Laura — once David confirms, can you join the validation call with Thalassa?

Thanks,
Aditya
Sales Engineering | Redwood Inference


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-14T10:01:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Steady-state throughput assurance — tech notes

Aditya — quick technical confirmation:

- 70% minimum: reasonable as a contractual floor for prioritized preemption tiers. Practically, we see short tail events where a small set of nodes can drop below that for a few minutes; the 10-minute cumulative rule for a missed bucket will protect customers from transient blips. If legal pushes back, we can add an explicit exclusions section for force-majeure / hypervisor-level maintenance.

- Emergency buffer: bumping to 20% is safer for initial commitment, especially since Thalassa wants a numeric SLA up-front. Our recommendation: propose 20% in the amendment, but include a 6‑month review clause that lets us re-evaluate buffer sizing after telemetry is live (so Thalassa can request a reduction if steady-state utilization proves stable).

I'll add a brief technical note to the workbook and mark the cell where we changed buffer from 15% -> 20%. Once that's in, Ben/Aditya — set up the 30-min validation call and invite Thalassa infra + procurement.

-David
Infra Reliability | Redwood Inference
text:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T09:12:00-04:00
Subject: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Hi Ellen —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. As we talked through on the demo, the primary commercial question you're raising is whether Redwood can include a steady-state throughput assurance for the reserved pool that backs your inference service (THL-Prod). Specifically: a commitment around sustained tokens/sec for steady workloads and explicit headroom rules for preemption/eviction during multi-tenant bursts.

I've sketched a conservative SLO amendment that:
- Guarantees 95th-percentile steady throughput at a committed concurrency profile (attached).
- Defines preemption windows and a 72-hr notice for planned maintenance that impacts reserved capacity.
- Specifies fallback routing (regional failover + lower-precision quantized model variants) in the event of capacity shortfalls.

Can you confirm whether the key ask from procurement is: (a) a numeric sustained throughput figure + credits for missed windows, or (b) a qualitative commitment (e.g., \"reasonable efforts + escalation playbook\")? We can draft either — the former requires slightly wider headroom planning on our side.

I've cc'd Laura (customer success) and Aditya (sales engineering) so they can align on the operational playbook if you want to proceed with a redline. If useful, I can attach a short throughput‑modeling workbook for your infra team to validate assumptions.

Next steps: if procurement wants a numeric SLA, we propose we send a formal amendment + the throughput model and run a quick joint validation with your infra (30–45 mins).

Thanks —

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
Attachment stubs: thalassa_sla_amendment_v1.pdf (draft), throughput_modeling_v1.xlsx (model)


From: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@thalassa.io, Marco Ruiz <m.ruiz@thalassa.io>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T11:05:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Ben —

Appreciate the quick follow-up and the draft. A few clarifying questions from our side before we bring legal in:

1) Numeric target — when you say "95th-percentile steady throughput at a committed concurrency profile", can you give an example figure and the concurrency assumptions? We need numbers to map onto our cost model.
2) Credits — how are missed windows measured? Rolling 24h windows or discrete outage events?
3) Isolation — will our reserved pool be fully isolated for scheduled capacity (no noisy neighbors), or is it a soft reservation with priority?
4) Fallback behavior — what is the expected latency/quality delta if you route to a quantized variant? We need an SLA that preserves core product quality for our end-users.

If you can answer these at a high level we can decide whether procurement takes the draft to legal or if we route to infra for validation first.

— Ellen Park
Head of Platform Ops | Thalassa Analytics
ellen.park@thalassa.io


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ellen Park <ellen.park@thalassa.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-13T08:30:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Steady-state throughput assurance — amendment request

Ellen — thanks, good questions. Quick answers below and attached a slightly expanded amendment + the modeling workbook so your infra/procurement can poke at numbers: thalassa_sla_amendment_v1.pdf, throughput_modeling_v1.xlsx.

1) Numeric example: for a committed concurrency of 40 steady sessions (avg token generation 40 tok/sec/session with 1.5x burst headroom), the model projects a sustainable nominal throughput of ~2,400 tok/sec at the 95th percentile. We can treat this as a template — if you want 60 sessions we update the numbers accordingly. The workbook includes a toggled table.

2) Measurement: we propose discrete missed-window measurements measured against 1-hour evaluation buckets across the billing day. A missed window is when sustained throughput for that 1-hour bucket falls below 85% of the committed nominal for >10 minutes (cumulative). Credits are pro-rated per missed bucket. If you'd prefer rolling 24h windows, we can update language.

3) Isolation: reserved pool == soft isolation with priority reservation. Practically: your workloads run on reserved nodes with preemption protection for certain throttles; however we reserve a small shared emergency buffer for platform health events. The amendment defines preemption tiers and a guaranteed minimum of 70% of the committed steady throughput even during prioritized preemption events (subject to scheduled maintenance windows).

4) Fallback latency/quality: routing to the quantized variant will typically add 10–30ms of tail latency on inference and may reduce token-level perplexity slightly for long-form generation. We call out an "acceptable quality window" in the amendment: business-critical prompts (flagged via API metadata) will not be routed to lower-precision variants unless explicitly authorized in the runbook.

If those points look reasonable, propose we run a 30–45 minute infra validation session (aditya + laura + your infra contact) to align on the numbers and then move the amendment to procurement/legal. When would be a good time for Thalassa?

Best,

Ben
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-13T14:20:00-04:00
Subject: Fwd: Steady-state throughput assurance — we need a tech sanity check

Forwarding Ben's reply to loop you in — Thalassa liked the shape of our answer but procurement will want the numbers validated. David, can you confirm two things for the modeling team:

- The 70% minimum during prioritized preemption events is conservative enough given current preemption mechanics on r8-reserved nodes (NVIDIA). Any recommended language changes?
- The emergency buffer sizing: the workbook assumes a 15% platform buffer shared across customers for short system windows; is 15% safe or should we use 20% for conservative quoting?

I'll put the workbook in the shared drive after you confirm; we want to avoid back-and-forth with procurement. Laura — once David confirms, can you join the validation call with Thalassa?

Thanks,
Aditya
Sales Engineering | Redwood Inference


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-14T10:01:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Steady-state throughput assurance — tech notes

Aditya — quick technical confirmation:

- 70% minimum: reasonable as a contractual floor for prioritized preemption tiers. Practically, we see short tail events where a small set of nodes can drop below that for a few minutes; the 10-minute cumulative rule for a missed bucket will protect customers from transient blips. If legal pushes back, we can add an explicit exclusions section for force-majeure / hypervisor-level maintenance.

- Emergency buffer: bumping to 20% is safer for initial commitment, especially since Thalassa wants a numeric SLA up-front. Our recommendation: propose 20% in the amendment, but include a 6‑month review clause that lets us re-evaluate buffer sizing after telemetry is live (so Thalassa can request a reduction if steady-state utilization proves stable).

I'll add a brief technical note to the workbook and mark the cell where we changed buffer from 15% -> 20%. Once that's in, Ben/Aditya — set up the 30-min validation call and invite Thalassa infra + procurement.

-David
Infra Reliability | Redwood Inference

Thanks for the intake and for drafting the MAP. Carlos and I reviewed the milestones and we'd like to proceed but we need to confirm a couple of constraints before you ship the prompt pack and dataset: 

- Usage scope: The data and prompt examples will be used strictly for evaluation within Prospect Labs and our contracted vendors; no public redistribution.

We can sign an NDA if needed; I've attached a countersigned one for speed. Can you confirm Redwood's standard evaluation license and whether you require any additional paperwork? Also, what's the ETA for the prompt pack delivery once we execute the MAP?

- Retention: 90 days post-delivery is fine; we'll mark automated deletion in the MAP and confirm deletion with a final audit log export.
